item.title: The AI Boom's Real Bottleneck Is the Power Grid — and Summer 2026 Is the Stress Test
item.body:
For two decades, American electricity demand barely budged. Efficiency gains soaked up growth, and utilities planned for a flat future. That era is over. The artificial intelligence buildout — hyperscale data centers stuffed with power-hungry GPUs — has turned electricity into the scarcest resource in tech, and this summer's heat is putting the collision between AI and the grid on full display.

## From megawatts to gigawatts

A single large AI training campus can draw as much power as a mid-sized city. Projects announced over the past two years — OpenAI's Stargate campuses, Meta's multi-gigawatt "Hyperion" and "Prometheus" sites, xAI's Colossus complex in Memphis — are measured not in megawatts but gigawatts. Analysts project data centers could roughly double their share of U.S. electricity consumption by 2030. The constraint on AI progress is no longer chips or capital. It is interconnection: the years-long queue to plug new load and new generation into a grid that wasn't built for this.

## Why summer makes it worse

Peak electricity demand in most of the country arrives on the hottest afternoons of July and August, when air conditioners and data centers compete for the same electrons. Grid operators like PJM — which covers the data center heartland of Virginia, the densest concentration of server farms on Earth — have seen capacity auction prices hit record levels, costs that flow directly into household utility bills. Rising residential rates in data center-heavy regions have turned grid strain from an engineering problem into a kitchen-table political issue.

## The scramble for firm power

The industry's response has been a historic pivot toward any source of round-the-clock electricity it can find:

- **Nuclear is back.** Michigan's Palisades plant became the first shuttered U.S. nuclear station ever restarted, and Three Mile Island's Unit 1 is being revived as the Crane Clean Energy Center under a landmark deal to power Microsoft's data centers. Tech giants have signed a wave of agreements backing small modular reactors, though most won't deliver power until the 2030s.
- **Gas turbines are sold out.** Order backlogs at major turbine manufacturers now stretch years into the future — meaning even companies willing to burn gas can't get the hardware quickly.
- **Behind-the-meter everything.** Developers are increasingly building generation on-site — gas, solar, batteries — to bypass the interconnection queue entirely.

## Efficiency: the quiet counterforce

There is a genuine engineering race on the demand side, too. Each new generation of AI accelerators delivers dramatically more computation per watt, liquid cooling is replacing air conditioning in server halls, and inference — the everyday running of AI models — is being optimized relentlessly because electricity is now a line item that shows up in every AI company's margins. The open question is a classic one: whether efficiency gains reduce total consumption, or simply make it cheap enough to build even more.

## Why it matters for everyone else

The grid crunch is quietly reshaping the map of American industry. States that can offer fast permits, transmission capacity, land, and water are winning tens of billions of dollars in investment; states that can't are watching it pass by. And for ordinary ratepayers, the central policy fight of the next few years is already visible: who pays for the grid the AI era demands — the companies driving the demand, or everyone else's monthly bill. This summer's heat waves are the first real-world exam. The results will shape how, and where, the next decade of computing gets built.
